attributes.stem:
A 72-year-old woman is brought by her son to be reviewed by her general practitioner. Over the last two years the son has noticed substantial memory impairment in his mother. She often forgets to turn the stove off and cannot always remember what day of the week it is. Yesterday he found her wandering in the street in her nightgown. Her medical history includes asthma, for which she uses an inhaler. She is not on any other medications; does not smoke and drinks only a glass or two of wine at weekends.

On examination her blood pressure is 132/84 mmHg, pulse 76/min and temperature 37.2oC. The physical examination, including a neurological and cardiovascular assessment, is unremarkable. She scores 18 out of 30 on the Folstein Mini–Mental Status Examination.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate plan of management?
attributes.explanation:
The patient's symptoms are suggestive of Alzheimer disease. Whilst this condition is not treatable, it is important to exclude other possible causes of dementia that might be ameliorated. Some form of brain imaging will be appropriate to avoid missing conditions that could be treated with a reasonable expectation of substantial recovery.

Current guidelines suggest that imaging of the brain with either a noncontrast head CT or MRI scan is appropriate in the standard initial evaluation of a patient with dementia. This imaging will serve two purposes. First to determine if there are any other potential causes of cognitive changes, such as subdural haematoma, tumour or normal-pressure hydrocephalus. Second, the scan will look for changes consistent with Alzheimer disease. These changes might include mesial temporal/hippocampal atrophy or posterior parietal atrophy. An MRI scan is the preferred imaging modality because it provides a more sensitive evaluation than a CT scan, particularly for identifying other forms of dementia, patterns of atrophy, and subtle vascular changes.

Observation and no investigations are not the appropriate plan of management for this patient because her symptoms are significant enough to warrant further evaluation. Her cognitive impairment is significantly affecting her daily functioning and puts her at risk for harm, as evidenced by her wandering in the street in her nightgown therefore investigating any treatable cause is necessary.

Carotid ultrasonography is not a useful investigation in this context because it primarily evaluates the blood flow in the carotid arteries and is not typically used for evaluating cognitive impairment. While carotid artery disease can be a risk factor for cognitive impairment, it is unlikely to be the primary cause in this case given the patient's medical history and symptoms.

Electroencephalogram (EEG) is not typically used to diagnose cognitive impairment unless the patient has a history of seizures or other neurological symptoms. EEG is a useful tool in the diagnosis and management of epilepsy, but it is not typically used as a first-line investigation for cognitive impairment.

Complete blood examination might indicate nutritional deficiency that may be causing cognitive impairment, but it would not be diagnostic and hence is not the most appropriate plan of management.
learningPoints: MRI is the best diagnostic imaging modality in a patient with cognitive impairment.
references: Arvanitakis Z, Shah RC, Bennett DA. Diagnosis and Management of Dementia: Review. JAMA. 2019 Oct 22;322(16):1589-1599.
